mainText:
## Domestic Terror on the Rise

### Countries Turn Down Call to Help in Strait

After claiming on Sunday that he would soon announce a coalition of countries prepared to escort merchant vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been blocked to merchant shipping by Iranian threats, President Donald Trump said at the White House on Monday that he was receiving mixed responses from other countries to the U.S. request for aid. “Some are very enthusiastic about it, and some aren’t,” he stated. Germany and Italy have completely ruled out taking military action to reopen passage through the Strait, while Britain and France have agreed only to conditional involvement. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the U.K. “will not be drawn into the wider war” but noted that British mine-hunting drones are already deployed. France pledged frigates for “purely defensive” escorts. All four countries deployed naval assets to the Mediterranean and the Red Sea at the beginning of the conflict. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi also stated her country has no current plans to deploy naval assets to the region.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated Monday that the U.S. was allowing Iranian oil tankers to transit through the Strait to preserve global oil supply. Additionally, Axios reported that direct communications between White House diplomatic envoy Steve Witkoff and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi had been reestablished.

### Court Blocks HHS Vaccine Decisions

A federal court on Monday blocked multiple vaccine policy decisions made by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., temporarily reversing decisions made by his appointees to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). Judge Brian E. Murphy of the U.S. District Court for Massachusetts found there was a “substantial likelihood” that Kennedy’s appointments to ACIP were made without observing proper federal procedures and violated the Federal Advisory Committee Act. He also found that the January 2026 vaccine schedule likely violated the Administrative Procedure Act. Murphy stayed the ACIP appointments and barred the committee from making any further vaccine policy decisions until the suit was resolved, leading to the postponement of ACIP’s scheduled meeting for this week. The government indicated it will appeal the ruling.

Murphy also stated in response to the government’s argument that ACIP had virtually unlimited authority to make recommendations, even if those recommendations suggested that children be infected with measles rather than receive the vaccine, that “the Court disagrees.” Over three meetings in 2025, the newly constituted ACIP had rescinded recommendations for multiple childhood vaccines, including for Hepatitis B, previously recommended for all newborns. Separately, in January, the CDC reduced its recommended immunizations for children from 17 to 11 diseases, excluding rotavirus, COVID, and the seasonal flu.

### Israel Expands Lebanon Operation

Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz announced that the Israel Defense Forces have begun a ground maneuver in Lebanon to remove threats and protect the residents of the Galilee and the north, expanding their air campaign earlier this month against the terrorist group Hezbollah in Lebanon’s south. Since the onset of the ongoing war against Iran, Hezbollah has launched over 1,000 rockets and drones at northern Israel. In retaliation, Israel has struck hundreds of Hezbollah targets in Lebanon, including Beirut, leading to the evacuation orders for large swaths of southern Lebanon, causing roughly 800,000 people to leave their homes.

A Hezbollah rocket attack on the Israeli town of Nahariyah wounded six people on Monday evening. Direct talks between Israel and the Lebanese government, with U.S. involvement, are reportedly expected in the coming days.

### Afghanistan and Pakistan Continue Strikes

Pakistan and Afghanistan have continued to exchange strikes for the third consecutive week as the border conflict shows no sign of abating. The Taliban government of Afghanistan accused Pakistan of attacking a hospital in Kabul treating drug addicts, asserting that the strike resulted in at least 400 fatalities and 250 injuries.
